name: Multi-Agent Systems
scope: A person with INTERMEDIATE proficiency level in Multi-Agent Systems as a competency should be able to explain agent and MAS fundamentals (agent properties, MAS types, environments, use cases), decompose problems into agents, roles, and interaction protocols, and design/implement core agent architectures (reactive, deliberative, BDI, hybrid, LLM-based patterns like planner–executor, tool-using agents). Should specify agent state, goals, policies, and actions; manage agent lifecycle, memory, and separation of concerns; implement direct, broadcast, and environment-mediated communication using schemas (JSON, tool call specs), FIPA ACL concepts, and handle coordination (task allocation, manager–worker, consensus, deadlock avoidance). Should apply planning and decision-making (centralized/decentralized planning, utility-based choices, heuristics, basic game theory, uncertainty handling, verification loops), and understand basics of multi-agent RL (MARL paradigms, PettingZoo, RLlib, reward structures, CTDE). Should model environments (state, actions, observability), design and simulate MAS tasks (using Mesa, NetLogo, custom envs), and evaluate agent/system behavior via metrics and logs. Should architect MAS applications (agent roles, orchestrators, memory, tool/API integration, vector DBs like Pinecone/Qdrant, event-driven systems, orchestration trade-offs), implement reliability and safety (policy/checker agents, allow/deny lists, human-in-the-loop, monitoring, alignment), and optimize for performance/scalability/observability (horizontal scaling, sharding, batching, logging, tracing, debugging). Should use and extend LLM agent frameworks (LangChain, LangGraph, AutoGen, crewAI), workflow engines (Temporal, Dagster, Prefect), agent platforms (JADE, SPADE), and integrate foundational AI services (LLM APIs, RAG, sandboxed code execution). Should containerize agents (Docker, Kubernetes), manage config/secrets, apply CI/CD and testing (unit, integration, scenario tests), and document MAS designs (diagrams, APIs, runbooks). Should collaborate with ML/data/product teams, communicate MAS architectures and trade-offs, participate in operational excellence (SLIs/SLOs, incident response, postmortems), and contribute to iterative improvement and best practices using evaluation data and feedback.
long_scope:
{"sections":[{"name":"Core Multi-Agent Systems Fundamentals","scope_included":"- Understand the definition of an agent, multi-agent system (MAS), and how MAS differs from single-agent and traditional distributed systems
- Explain key properties of agents: autonomy, reactivity, proactiveness, social ability, learning capability, and bounded rationality
- Distinguish between:
  - Cooperative vs competitive vs mixed-motive MAS
  - Centralized vs decentralized vs hybrid control
  - Homogeneous vs heterogeneous agent populations
- Understand environments in MAS:
  - Fully vs partially observable
  - Deterministic vs stochastic
  - Static vs dynamic vs semi-dynamic
  - Discrete vs continuous state/action spaces
- Understand fundamental MAS use cases and where they’re appropriate:
  - LLM-based multi-agent orchestration (tool use, workflows, copilots)
  - Robotics and swarm behavior
  - Distributed resource allocation and scheduling
  - Multi-agent simulation for planning, markets, and policy
- Be able to decompose a problem into:
  - Environment/state representation
  - Agent types and roles
  - Interaction protocols
  - Coordination and conflict-resolution mechanisms
- Recognize common MAS performance and quality dimensions:
  - Task success/utility
  - Robustness and fault tolerance
  - Scalability with number of agents
  - Communication overhead and latency
  - Alignment with human goals and constraints","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Agent Architectures & Internal Design","scope_included":"- Understand and work with core agent architectures:
  - Reactive agents (rule-based, stimulus–response)
  - Deliberative agents (symbolic, planning-based)
  - BDI (Belief–Desire–Intention) agents
  - Hybrid architectures (reactive + deliberative layers)
- For LLM-centric systems, understand and design:
  - Prompt-based agent personalities, roles, and capabilities
  - Planner–executor, manager–worker, and critic–solver patterns
  - Tool-using agents (function calling, tool APIs, retrieval, code execution)
- Be able to specify an agent formally in terms of:
  - Belief/state representation
  - Goal/utility function or reward signal
  - Decision policy (rules, heuristics, learned policy, LLM prompting)
  - Action space (including external APIs, tools, and environment effects)
- Implement mid-complexity agents using mainstream technologies:
  - LLM-based agents (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) with function calling or similar interfaces
  - Rule-based agents (e.g., Python + rule engines) for simple reactive logic
  - Simple BDI-style agents (e.g., using frameworks like Jason, JADEX, or custom implementations)
- Design and implement agent life-cycle management:
  - Initialization, configuration, and context bootstrapping
  - Maintaining short-term and long-term state/memory
  - Handling retries, backoff, and error states in agent control loops
- Apply separation of concerns within an agent:
  - Reasoning / decision logic vs tool execution vs state management
  - Prompt templates / policies vs runtime data
  - Domain logic vs infrastructure concerns (I/O, logging, metrics)","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Inter-Agent Communication & Coordination","scope_included":"- Understand modes of communication:
  - Direct agent-to-agent messaging
  - Broadcast and publish–subscribe patterns
  - Shared memory / blackboard systems
  - Environment-mediated communication (stigmergy, shared artifacts)
- Understand basics of agent communication languages and protocols:
  - Core ideas in FIPA ACL, performatives (request, inform, propose, reject, agree)
  - Message structure (sender, receiver(s), content, ontology, conversation-id)
- Design and implement communication for mid-sized systems:
  - Routing strategies (direct addressing, topics, roles)
  - Conversation state and correlation IDs
  - Timeouts, retries, and dead-letter handling
- Implement coordination patterns:
  - Task allocation (e.g., contract net-style: announce–bid–award; though possibly simplified)
  - Leader/facilitator agents for orchestration
  - Manager–worker, peer-to-peer, and marketplace-style interactions
  - Consensus via simple protocols (majority vote, mediator/arbiter agents)
- For LLM-based systems, design structured communication:
  - Use consistent schemas for messages (JSON payloads, tool call specs)
  - Encode role, goals, and context within prompts and message envelopes
  - Manage conversation history length and summarization between agents
- Understand coordination challenges and mitigations:
  - Deadlocks and livelocks
  - Thrashing and redundant work
  - Cascading errors from miscommunication
  - Conflicting instructions from multiple agents","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Reasoning, Planning, and Decision-Making in MAS","scope_included":"- Understand core planning concepts in a multi-agent context:
  - Centralized planning with decentralized execution vs fully decentralized planning
  - Joint plans vs individual plans with coordination constraints
  - Temporal and resource constraints across agents
- For LLM-based MAS:
  - Design multi-step reasoning flows (chain-of-thought, tree-of-thought, and reflection loops) across multiple agents
  - Use specialized agents for planning, critic/review, execution, and verification
  - Encode and enforce task decomposition, subgoals, and dependencies
- Apply basic decision-making methods:
  - Utility-based decisions (selecting among alternative plans/actions)
  - Heuristic search in joint or factored state spaces
  - Rule-based policies and priority schemes
- Understand and apply basic game-theoretic intuition:
  - Cooperative vs competitive objectives and implications for policies
  - Conceptual understanding of Nash equilibrium, Pareto efficiency (not rigorous proofs)
  - Incentive compatibility and free-rider issues in cooperative MAS
- Design decision policies under uncertainty:
  - Handling partial observability and missing information
  - Using confidence/uncertainty (e.g., LLM logprobs, model self-assessment) to trigger verification or escalation
  - Safeguards against hallucinations and overconfident agents
- Implement verification and validation loops:
  - Cross-checking between agents (solver vs verifier/corrector)
  - Redundancy and voting for high-risk decisions
  - Fallback policies when consensus is not reached","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Multi-Agent Learning and Reinforcement Learning (Introductory Depth)","scope_included":"- Understand conceptual differences between single-agent RL and multi-agent RL (MARL):
  - Non-stationarity of the environment from each agent’s perspective
  - Joint vs independent learning, and resulting convergence issues
  - Credit assignment across agents
- Know basic MARL paradigms conceptually:
  - Independent Q-learning agents and their limitations
  - Centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE)
  - Cooperative vs competitive vs mixed MARL settings
- Be able to reason about where MARL is vs isn’t appropriate for real-world systems
- Use existing MARL frameworks at a practitioner level (not research-level):
  - Understand environment interfaces such as PettingZoo or multi-agent Gym
  - Run and adapt basic MARL examples via RLlib, MARLlib, or similar
  - Configure simple multi-agent policies and reward structures
- Design reward structures in cooperative MAS:
  - Shaped vs global rewards
  - Balancing individual vs team rewards to encourage desired behaviors
- For LLM-based systems, understand interaction with learning:
  - Prompt/agent configuration as a form of policy design
  - Where fine-tuning vs prompt-engineering vs explicit RL might be applied
  - Use of feedback data (human or automated) to iteratively improve multi-agent workflows","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Environment Modeling, Simulation, and Task Design","scope_included":"- Represent MAS environments clearly:
  - State variables, entities, and relationships
  - Action models (preconditions and effects)
  - Observations and partial observability per agent type
- Design task scenarios for MAS:
  - Cooperative tasks with shared goals and deadlines
  - Tasks requiring role specialization and hand-offs
  - Conflict scenarios (resource contention, conflicting goals) to test coordination
- Use simulation frameworks and libraries:
  - Agent-based simulation tools (e.g., Mesa in Python, NetLogo for conceptual understanding)
  - Multi-agent RL/simulation environments (e.g., PettingZoo, custom gym-like envs)
- For LLM-based MAS in software/knowledge domains:
  - Model virtual environments via APIs, tools, and knowledge sources
  - Design “world models” as structured contexts: documents, databases, vector stores, codebases
  - Simulate user interactions and edge cases in sandbox environments
- Evaluate MAS behavior through simulation:
  - Run repeated episodes with varied initial conditions
  - Collect metrics (success rates, time to completion, resource usage, error rates)
  - Inspect and interpret logs of inter-agent communication and decisions","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Systems Design & Architecture of Multi-Agent Applications","scope_included":"- Decompose real-world problems into MAS architectures:
  - Define agent roles, capabilities, and boundaries
  - Decide when to use single complex agent vs multiple specialized agents
  - Identify necessary shared services (memory, tools, knowledge bases, orchestrators)
- Design reference architectures for mid-sized MAS applications:
  - Orchestrator/manager agent supervising specialist agents
  - Marketplace of service agents with a router/dispatcher
  - Hierarchical agent structures (meta-agents overseeing sub-teams)
- Integrate agents with external systems:
  - Tool and API integration (HTTP services, databases, vector stores, code runners)
  - Event-driven architectures using queues/streaming (e.g., Kafka, RabbitMQ, cloud pub/sub)
  - Integration with existing backend services and workflows
- Manage state and memory across agents:
  - Short-term vs long-term memory
  - Shared vs private memory per agent
  - Use of vector databases (e.g., Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, Chroma) for retrieval
  - Summarization strategies to keep memory bounded
- Handle orchestration concerns:
  - Task routing, prioritization, and scheduling among agents
  - Timeouts, retries, and circuit breakers for unreliable tools and agents
  - Idempotency of actions where appropriate (e.g., when agents re-run tasks)
- Make trade-offs explicit:
  - More agents vs more complex single agent
  - Centralized orchestration vs decentralized autonomy
  - Latency vs quality of results vs cost (compute, tokens, network)","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Reliability, Safety, and Alignment in Multi-Agent Systems","scope_included":"- Recognize MAS-specific failure modes:
  - Emergent undesirable behaviors (collusion, deadlocks, feedback loops)
  - Error propagation and amplification through agent chains
  - Over-dependence on a single orchestrator or agent type
- Implement safety and guardrail mechanisms:
  - Policy/checker/guardian agents that review actions or messages
  - Allow/deny lists for tools and external actions
  - Rate-limiting and budget constraints (API tokens, money, resources)
- Design human-in-the-loop control points:
  - Human approval gates for high-risk actions
  - Escalation workflows when agents are uncertain or disagree
  - Clear audit trails to support post-hoc review
- Apply alignment principles in MAS:
  - Ensure agent goals are aligned with overall system and organizational goals
  - Avoid reward hacking and perverse incentives in cooperative setups
  - Constrain agent autonomy in safety-critical domains
- Design and use monitoring for safety:
  - Logs of messages, tool calls, and critical decisions
  - Detection of anomalous behavior (e.g., unusually long loops, repeated failures)
  - Alerts for breaches of predefined safety policies","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Performance, Scalability, and Observability in MAS","scope_included":"- Understand key performance concerns in MAS:
  - Latency and throughput across agent workflows
  - Communication overhead vs useful work
  - Bottlenecks (orchestrator agents, shared resources, tools/APIs)
- Reason about scalability strategies:
  - Horizontal scaling of stateless agents
  - Sharding of agent populations by user, domain, or task
  - Load balancing across agent instances and tool backends
- Implement performance-conscious designs:
  - Limit and structure agent interactions to avoid combinatorial explosion
  - Use caching of intermediate results and external API responses
  - Batch requests where possible (e.g., batched LLM calls or DB operations)
- Implement observability:
  - Logging key events: task assignments, tool calls, agent decisions
  - Collecting metrics (latency per step, cost per workflow, error rates, agent utilization)
  - Basic tracing of workflows across agents (correlation IDs, spans)
- Perform targeted debugging and performance tuning:
  - Reproduce problematic multi-agent episodes in a controlled environment
  - Use logs and traces to identify miscoordination or misconfiguration
  - Adjust prompts, agent roles, or orchestration logic based on observed issues","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Tooling, Frameworks, and Infrastructure for Multi-Agent Systems","scope_included":"- Use and extend modern LLM multi-agent frameworks (practitioner depth):
  - Orchestration frameworks (e.g., LangChain agents, LangGraph, Microsoft AutoGen, crewAI, open-source agent toolkits)
  - Workflow engines/state machines (e.g., Temporal, Step Functions, Dagster, Prefect) to manage complex agent workflows
- Use general-purpose MAS platforms where appropriate:
  - Agent platforms such as JADE, SPADE, or similar (at least conceptual and basic hands-on familiarity)
  - Understand how they handle containers for agents, messaging, and discovery
- Work with environment and MARL tooling:
  - PettingZoo or similar multi-agent environment libraries
  - RLlib or comparable libraries for multi-agent RL experiments
  - Simple agent-based simulation tools (e.g., Mesa) for prototyping
- Integrate foundational AI services and infrastructure:
  - LLM APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.), embeddings, vector DBs
  - Tooling for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) in agent workflows
  - Sandboxed code execution environments (e.g., Docker-based sandboxes, cloud functions) used by coding agents
- Implement deployment and runtime management:
  - Containerization of agents/services (Docker) and basic orchestration (e.g., Kubernetes, serverless platforms)
  - Configuration management for agent parameters, prompts, tools, and policies
  - Use of secrets and credentials management for tools/APIs (e.g., Vault, cloud secret managers)
- Apply basic CI/CD and testing practices for MAS:
  - Automated tests for agent behaviors and interactions (unit, integration, scenario tests)
  - Synthetic scenarios and regression suites to guard against unintended emergent behavior changes","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Collaboration, Documentation, and Operational Practices","scope_included":"- Communicate MAS designs clearly to technical and non-technical stakeholders:
  - Describe agent roles, responsibilities, and interaction flows
  - Explain trade-offs and limitations of chosen architectures
- Produce and maintain documentation:
  - Architecture diagrams showing agents, environment, tools, and communication channels
  - Interface contracts (APIs/tool schemas, message formats, ontologies)
  - Operational runbooks for incident handling and debugging MAS
- Collaborate effectively in a team building MAS:
  - Work with data/ML engineers on environment, reward design, and MARL experiments
  - Work with product and domain experts to define agent roles and constraints
  - Review and contribute to design proposals and RFCs for MAS features
- Participate in operational excellence:
  - Help define SLIs/SLOs for MAS behavior (latency, reliability, quality metrics)
  - Participate in on-call/incident response related to MAS components
  - Perform post-incident reviews focusing on systemic MAS issues (coordination, safety, scaling)
- Contribute to iterative improvement:
  - Use evaluation data, logs, and user feedback to refine agent prompts, roles, and workflows
  - Propose experiments to validate changes to multi-agent strategies
  - Help establish internal patterns and best practices for MAS within the organization","is_non_core_section":true}]}
